---
title: "Tutorials"
description: "Worked examples using the EpiAware packages together."
---

Get started with one of two complementary approaches, then explore the
in-depth tutorials below.

## Approaches

Two ways of working with the EpiAware ecosystem — start with whichever fits
your question.

| Approach | What it does |
|---|---|
| [Compose distributions](approaches/composed-distributions.qmd) | Model a case's progression as a tree of delays using `Sequential`, `Parallel`, `Resolve`, and `Compete`. Starting point for delay estimation and event simulation. |
| [Assemble an infectious disease model](approaches/assembled-model.qmd) | Build a joint model from a latent process, infection mechanism, and observation layers. Starting point for inference and forecasting. |

## In-depth tutorials

Tutorials that combine the packages in a full analysis are in development.
Each package's own documentation includes worked examples — see the
[Packages](packages/index.qmd) page.

[CensoredDistributions.jl](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/) has
the most complete set, including the
[BDBV line-list analysis](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/getting-started/tutorials/bdbv-linelist-analysis/)
— a Bayesian workflow that builds one composed distribution for the whole case,
fits it, and checks recovery against published estimates.

The [Bayesian Workflow](https://avehtari.github.io/Bayesian-Workflow/) book and
[a workflow for infectious disease modelling](https://github.com/seabbs/a-workflow-for-infectious-disease-modelling)
give the modelling approach these tools are designed to support.

Have a tutorial you'd like to see, or one you'd like to write? Suggest it on the
[forum](https://community.epinowcast.org/) or see [Contributing](contributing.qmd).
